title: Polytopes and C0 -Riemannian Metrics with Positive Topological Entropy
speaker: Marcelo Alves
abstract:
The topological entropy of geodesic flows has been extensively studied since the foundational works of Dinaburg and Manning. It measures the exponential complexity of the geodesic flow of a Riemannian manifold, and there are several results connecting it to the geometry and topology of a Riemannian manifold. In this talk I will explain how recent results obtained jointly with Dahinden, Meiwes, and Pirnapasov can be used to give a meaningful extension of the topological entropy to 
C
0
-Riemannian metrics; i.e. Riemannian metrics which are continuous but not necessarily differentiable. Similarly, using contact geometry I will explain how we can talk in a meaningful way about the topological entropy of convex and starshaped polytopes in 
R
4
, thinking of them as 
C
0
-contact forms. This is joint work with Matthias Meiwes.

proof_stages.label: Open-cover definition of topological entropy
proof_stages.summary: The talk first fixes topological entropy as the exponential growth rate of the minimal number of refined open-cover words needed to code all orbit segments of a continuous self-map.

proof_stages.label: C0 contact forms and graphical DC0 geometry
proof_stages.summary: Continuous defining one-forms for a contact structure are equipped with the DC0 distance, which is identified with graphical distance between contact-type hypersurfaces in a symplectization, allowing continuous star-shaped hypersurfaces such as polytopes to be read as C0 contact forms.

proof_stages.label: Entropy of a C0 contact form by smoothings
proof_stages.summary: For a continuous contact form alpha, the proposed entropy is the infimum over all smoothings of alpha of the liminf of the topological entropies of the corresponding smooth Reeb flows.

proof_stages.label: Contact-homological recovery of smooth Reeb entropy
proof_stages.summary: For generic smooth contact forms on three-manifolds, the topological entropy of the Reeb flow can be recovered as a limit of exponential homotopical growth rates of cylindrical contact homology in complements of increasingly complicated links of Reeb orbits.

proof_stages.label: DC0 lower semicontinuity of Reeb entropy
proof_stages.summary: Using holomorphic cylinders and neck-stretching under DC0 perturbations, enough nonhomotopic Reeb orbits persist to prove a generic lower-semicontinuity theorem, so the smoothing entropy agrees with the usual Reeb-flow entropy on a C-infinity open dense set of smooth contact forms.

proof_stages.label: High-entropy star-shaped polytope theorem
proof_stages.summary: Star-shaped polytopes in R4 are placed in the C0 contact-form framework, and the main theorem asserts the existence of volume-one star-shaped, even convex, polytopes with arbitrarily large smoothing entropy.

proof_stages.label: Superlevel openness plus density of polytopes
proof_stages.summary: The polytope theorem follows because entropy superlevel sets are open and nonempty, using high-entropy smooth forms and generic stability, while star-shaped polytopes are dense in the DC0 topology by radial projection of fine triangulations to smooth star-shaped hypersurfaces, with volume then normalized.

proof_stages.label: C0 Riemannian metric analogue
proof_stages.summary: The same smoothing-infimum idea is applied to continuous Riemannian metrics, whose geodesic flow need not be defined, and the speaker states that many such C0 metrics have infinite entropy.

examples.name: Polygonal billiards and obstacle analogy
examples.note: Katok's zero-entropy result for convex polygonal billiards, contrasted with polygonal billiards with an obstacle, is used as an analogy for why vertices or nonconvex-like interactions might create entropy in polytope dynamics.
examples.engagement_reason: polygonal billiards and polygonal obstacles are invoked as an analogy via known entropy behavior, without working through a particular billiard trajectory or calculation

passes.reason: Polygonal billiards with an obstacle are used to refute the naive extension of zero entropy from convex polygonal billiards.
The polygonal-obstacle billiard is a standard nonconvex billiard example used to show the zero-entropy polygonal billiard intuition can fail.
Polygonal billiards with an obstacle are used to refute the zero-entropy heuristic suggested by convex polygonal billiards.
Polygonal billiards with an obstacle are used to show the zero-entropy convex-polygon analogy need not persist in the nonconvex/obstacle setting.
Polygonal billiards with an obstacle are used as an enemy case to the naive expectation that polygonal dynamics should have zero entropy.
